note_data.title: Financial statements – Part A
keyPoints: The process of accounting starts once the book-keeper finishes recording the financial transactions
It deals with measuring, identifying, and communicating financial information recorded by the book-keeper which facilitates the preparation of financial statements
Bookkeeping involves recording financial transactions from source documents of a business whereas accounting deals with maintaining financial statements from the bookkeeping records
Assets are the resources owned and controlled by a company, with the formula: Assets = Capital + Liabilities
An increase in an asset is a debit and a decrease is a credit
Current Assets are assets which have a monetary value or can be converted into cash within 12 months
Non-Current Assets are also known as Fixed Assets, these assets are acquired to earn revenue for the company and used for more than 12 months without being intended for resale
Capital is the number of resources and funds provided by the owner injected into the business
An increase in capital is a credit while a decrease or drawings is a debit

definitions.definition: The resources owned and controlled by a company
An asset which has a monetary value or can be converted into cash within 12 months. Examples include account receivables and cash

Also known as Fixed Assets, these assets are acquired to earn revenue for the company and used for more than 12 months without being intended for resale. Examples include buildings and machinery, tools, and vehicles
The number of resources and funds provided by the owner injected into the business

Goodwill refers to the intangible asset that represents the reputation, customer loyalty, brand value, and other non-physical attributes of a business. It arises when one company acquires another company for a price higher than the fair value of its net assets

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Set out the full trading account/income statement with proper headings and layout
Show your working clearly and state the final answer with appropriate label

modelAnswer.question: Explain why closing inventory is deducted when calculating cost of sales. (3 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: Closing inventory is deducted because it represents goods that have been purchased but not yet sold. Cost of sales should only include the cost of goods that have actually been sold to customers during the period. By deducting closing inventory from opening inventory plus purchases, we arrive at the true cost of goods sold.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Closing inventory represents goods purchased but not sold
Mark 2: Cost of sales should only include goods actually sold
Mark 3: Deducting closing inventory gives true cost of goods sold

commonMistakes: Confusing bookkeeping with accounting - bookkeeping involves recording financial transactions while accounting deals with maintaining financial statements
Incorrectly applying debit and credit rules - increases in assets are debits, decreases are credits
Mixing up current and non-current assets classification based on the 12-month timeframe
examinerTips: Remember the basic accounting equation: Assets = Capital + Liabilities
Understand that goodwill may be subject to impairment if its value declines over time
formulas: Cost of sales = Opening inventory + Purchases - Closing inventory
Gross profit = Sales - Cost of sales
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Vertical format trading account showing Sales at top, less Cost of sales (with breakdown of opening inventory + purchases - closing inventory), equals Gross profit. Then Income statement showing Gross profit less Expenses equals Net profit.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q2 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare a Trading Account and calculate gross profit for a retail business given incomplete records
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q1 (6 marks) — candidates had to identify and correct errors in a Profit and Loss Account, focusing on expense classification
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (10 marks) — students needed to prepare a complete Income Statement from a trial balance, including adjustments for accruals and prepayments
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q2 (7 marks) — they tested knowledge of Balance Sheet preparation and the accounting equation with missing figures calculations

workedExample.question: From the following information, prepare the Trading Account for ABC Traders for the year ended 31 December 2023: Opening stock $8,000; Purchases $45,000; Purchase returns $2,000; Sales $75,000; Sales returns $3,000; Closing stock $12,000; Carriage inwards $1,500. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate Net Sales = Sales - Sales Returns = $75,000 - $3,000 = $72,000
Step 2: Calculate Net Purchases = Purchases - Purchase Returns + Carriage Inwards = $45,000 - $2,000 + $1,500 = $44,500
Step 3: Calculate Cost of Goods Sold = Opening Stock + Net Purchases - Closing Stock = $8,000 + $44,500 - $12,000 = $40,500
Step 4: Calculate Gross Profit = Net Sales - Cost of Goods Sold = $72,000 - $40,500 = $31,500
workedExample.answer:
ABC TRADERS TRADING ACCOUNT for year ended 31 December 2023
Sales: $75,000, Less: Sales Returns $3,000 = Net Sales $72,000
Less: Cost of Goods Sold - Opening Stock $8,000 + Purchases $45,000 + Carriage Inwards $1,500 - Purchase Returns $2,000 - Closing Stock $12,000 = $40,500
Gross Profit = $31,500
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct format/headings, 1 mark for net sales calculation, 2 marks for cost of goods sold calculation showing all components, 1 mark for correct closing stock treatment, 1 mark for correct gross profit

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused revenue expenditure with capital expenditure in the Profit and Loss Account' — always check whether items like equipment repairs (revenue) vs equipment purchases (capital) are correctly classified

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently omitted closing stock from Trading Account calculations' — always remember closing stock is DEDUCTED when calculating cost of goods sold, and appears as a current asset on the Balance Sheet

Examiner Report 2023: 'Poor presentation and incorrect headings cost many candidates marks' — always use proper format with business name, statement type, and period covered as the heading, plus ensure debits/credits or assets/liabilities are clearly separated
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the standard format for Trading and Profit & Loss Account showing: TOP SECTION (Trading Account) with Sales minus Sales Returns equals Net Sales on right side, and Cost of Goods Sold calculation (Opening Stock + Net Purchases - Closing Stock) on left side, with Gross Profit as balancing figure. BOTTOM SECTION (Profit & Loss Account) shows Gross Profit plus other income on right, all expenses listed on left, with Net Profit as final balancing figure. Both sections should be clearly labeled with proper headings and the accounting period.
note_data.quickRecap: Accounting starts after bookkeeping is complete and involves measuring, identifying, and communicating financial information to prepare financial statements. Assets are resources owned by a company and can be classified as current (convertible to cash within 12 months) or non-current (used for more than 12 months). The fundamental accounting equation is Assets = Capital + Liabilities, with specific debit and credit rules applying to each component.
